quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Electromagnetic wave structure"?

options.text: Objects underwater appear shallower than they are due to refraction: apparent depth = actual depth × (n₂/n₁)
Uses r = mv/(qB) to separate ions by mass: heavier ions curve less (larger r), lighter ions curve more (smaller r)
Oscillating E and B fields, perpendicular to each other and to the propagation direction; E × B points in the direction of travel
Series: R_total = R₁ + R₂; same current through each. Parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂; same voltage across each

quiz_questions.explanation: "Electromagnetic wave structure" is defined as: Oscillating E and B fields, perpendicular to each other and to the propagation direction; E × B points in the direction of travel. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Malus's law"?

options.text: The image from lens 1 serves as the object for lens 2; d_o2 = separation - d_i1; apply thin lens equation sequentially
P_total = P₁ + P₂; equivalent to adding the reciprocals of focal lengths
I = I₀ cos²θ; intensity of polarized light transmitted through an analyzer at angle θ to the polarization direction
ε = -L(dI/dt); a changing current in a coil induces an EMF in itself; L = inductance in henries (H)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Malus's law" is defined as: I = I₀ cos²θ; intensity of polarized light transmitted through an analyzer at angle θ to the polarization direction. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Brewster's angle"?

options.text: ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2; fundamental limit on simultaneous measurement precision; not due to instrument limitations but inherent to nature
Net E at a point = vector sum of fields from all individual charges; requires component-wise addition
f_observed = f_source · (v ± v_observer)/(v ∓ v_source); approaching gives higher pitch, receding gives lower pitch
θ_B = arctan(n₂/n₁); at this angle of incidence, reflected light is 100% polarized; reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular

quiz_questions.explanation: "Brewster's angle" is defined as: θ_B = arctan(n₂/n₁); at this angle of incidence, reflected light is 100% polarized; reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Wave-particle duality"?

options.text: Light behaves as waves (interference, diffraction) and particles (photoelectric effect, Compton scattering); complementary descriptions
Node at closed end, antinode at open: f_n = nv/(4L), n = 1,3,5,... (odd harmonics only); e.g., clarinet, stopped pipe
f_observed = f_source · (v ± v_observer)/(v ∓ v_source); approaching gives higher pitch, receding gives lower pitch
d_o > 0 (real object); d_i > 0 (real image, opposite side); d_i  0 (converging); f < 0 (diverging)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Wave-particle duality" is defined as: Light behaves as waves (interference, diffraction) and particles (photoelectric effect, Compton scattering); complementary descriptions. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Light as a Wave, which best defines "Polarization"?

options.text: Node at closed end, antinode at open: f_n = nv/(4L), n = 1,3,5,... (odd harmonics only); e.g., clarinet, stopped pipe
Restricting E field oscillations to one direction; only possible for transverse waves; confirms light's transverse nature
Two converging lenses (objective + eyepiece); total magnification M = m_objective × M_eyepiece; enlarges nearby small objects
Uses r = mv/(qB) to separate ions by mass: heavier ions curve less (larger r), lighter ions curve more (smaller r)

quiz_questions.explanation: In Light as a Wave, "Polarization" refers to: Restricting E field oscillations to one direction; only possible for transverse waves; confirms light's transverse nature.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Oscillating E and B fields, perpendicular to each other and to the propagation direction; E × B points in the direction of travel"?

options.text: Total internal reflection
Absorption spectrum
Sound intensity level
Electromagnetic wave structure

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Electromagnetic wave structure".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "I = I₀ cos²θ; intensity of polarized light transmitted through an analyzer at angle θ to the polarization direction"?

options.text: Mass spectrometer
Rydberg formula
Malus's law
Total internal reflection

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Malus's law".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "θ_B = arctan(n₂/n₁); at this angle of incidence, reflected light is 100% polarized; reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular"?

options.text: Brewster's angle
Snell's law
Electric current
Lens power

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Brewster's angle".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Light behaves as waves (interference, diffraction) and particles (photoelectric effect, Compton scattering); complementary descriptions"?

options.text: Curie temperature
Wave-particle duality
Lensmaker's equation
Standard Model

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Wave-particle duality".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Light as a Wave, which term matches: "Restricting E field oscillations to one direction; only possible for transverse waves; confirms light's transverse nature"?

options.text: Polarization
Second Law of Thermodynamics
Self-inductance
Heisenberg uncertainty principle

quiz_questions.explanation: In Light as a Wave, this refers to "Polarization".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Light as a Wave is accurate?

options.text: Nuclear stability depends on the neutron-to-proton ratio: light stable nuclei have N ≈ Z; heavier nuclei need more neutrons (N > Z).
Planck proposed that energy is emitted/absorbed in discrete quanta: E = nhf.
The electromagnetic force governs the behavior of all charged matter, from electrons in atoms to current in wires.
Light as a Wave examines the wave nature of light in detail.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Light as a Wave as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Light as a Wave?

options.text: Nuclear stability depends on the neutron-to-proton ratio: light stable nuclei have N ≈ Z; heavier nuclei need more neutrons (N > Z).
Field lines point away from positive charges and toward negative charges.
Electromagnetic waves consist of oscillating electric and magnetic fields perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation.
Mass and energy are interconvertible: the mass defect in nuclear reactions is converted to kinetic energy of products and radiation.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Light as a Wave as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Light as a Wave?

options.text: Polarization proves light is a transverse wave.
Heat engines convert thermal energy to work with efficiency η = W/Q_H = 1 - Q_C/Q_H.
Young's double-slit experiment demonstrates wave nature: constructive interference occurs at d sin θ = mλ (bright fringes, m = 0,±1,±2,...); destructive at d sin θ = (m+½)λ (dark fringes).
Pressure is defined as force per unit area: P = F/A.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Light as a Wave as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Light as a Wave?

options.text: Nuclear stability depends on the neutron-to-proton ratio: light stable nuclei have N ≈ Z; heavier nuclei need more neutrons (N > Z).
The dielectric reduces the electric field between plates and allows more charge to be stored at the same voltage.
Malus's law gives the intensity of polarized light through an analyzer: I = I₀ cos²θ.
Sign conventions must be applied consistently for accurate results.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Light as a Wave as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Light as a Wave?

options.text: Brewster's angle: reflected light is completely polarized when tan θ_B = n₂/n₁.
When E and B are perpendicular, a velocity selector filters particles: only those with v = E/B pass through undeflected (electric and magnetic forces balance).
Plane mirrors produce virtual, upright, same-size images located behind the mirror.
Applications of Quantum Ideas extends quantum principles to technologies and fundamental physics.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Light as a Wave as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Light as a Wave?

options.text: The Sun converts 600 million tons of hydrogen into 596 million tons of helium each second; the 4 million ton mass deficit becomes 3.8 × 10²⁶ W of radiant power via E = mc²
Car side mirrors are convex ('objects in mirror are closer than they appear') to provide a wider field of view, reducing blind spots
Medical X-rays: high-energy photons penetrate soft tissue but are absorbed by dense bone, creating shadow images for diagnosing fractures and other conditions
3D movies use two differently polarized images projected simultaneously; polarized glasses filter one image to each eye, creating stereoscopic depth perception

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Light as a Wave.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Light as a Wave?

options.text: Rutherford's experiment was done with gold because it's malleable enough to make foils only a few atoms thick—thicker foils would scatter too many alpha particles
Nuclear weapons: a 20-kiloton bomb converts only about 1 gram of matter to energy (E = mc² = 0.001 kg × 9 × 10¹⁶ = 9 × 10¹³ J ≈ 20 kilotons of TNT)
LCD displays: liquid crystal pixels rotate polarized light by varying amounts controlled by voltage; combined with polarizing filters, this modulates light transmission to create images
Neon signs: specific gases glow with characteristic colors because their unique energy level spacings produce photons at specific visible wavelengths

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Light as a Wave.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Light as a Wave applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Photoelectric solar cells: photon energy (E = hf) must exceed the work function of the material to free electrons and generate current—the particle nature of light in action
Phone chargers contain transformers (and rectifiers) that convert 120V AC to 5-20V DC for safe device charging
Household wiring: outlets are wired in parallel (each at 120V) with circuit breakers in series (trip at 15-20A) to prevent overheating and fires
Arrow of time: entropy increase explains why ice melts in warm rooms but warm rooms don't spontaneously freeze—the second law governs the direction of all natural processes

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Light as a Wave.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Light as a Wave?

options.text: 3D movies use two differently polarized images projected simultaneously; polarized glasses filter one image to each eye, creating stereoscopic depth perception
Nuclear power plants: controlled fission of ²³⁵U in fuel rods heats water to produce steam that drives turbines, generating ~20% of US electricity without direct CO₂ emissions
Radio reception: AM radio waves (λ ≈ 200-600m) diffract around buildings and mountains, while FM (λ ≈ 3m) requires more line-of-sight; why AM reaches farther in hilly terrain
Anti-reflective coatings on lenses use thin-film destructive interference: film thickness is chosen so reflected waves cancel, maximizing transmitted light

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Light as a Wave.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Light as a Wave in practice?

options.text: Weather balloons expand as they rise because atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude, and Boyle's law predicts volume increase at lower pressure
Laser systems: multi-lens arrangements expand, collimate, and focus laser beams with precise control for applications from surgery to manufacturing
Lightning: charge separation in thunderclouds creates enormous electric fields (≈3 × 10⁶ V/m) that ionize air and create conductive channels, equalizing charge via massive current discharge
LCD displays: liquid crystal pixels rotate polarized light by varying amounts controlled by voltage; combined with polarizing filters, this modulates light transmission to create images

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Light as a Wave.
